en: How strange it is that I am being grouped with him who never evinced briskness of pace like me nor had he to his credit any achievement like mine unless he claims something of which I do not know and which I think Allāh too does not know. In any case, all praise belongs to Allāh.

t: To Muawiya
ar: واما ما سالت من دفع قتله عثمان اليك, فاني نظرت في هذا الامر, فلم اره يسعني دفعهم اليك ولا الي غيرك, ولعمري لين لم تنزع عن غيك وشقاقك لتعرفنهم عن قليل يطلبونك, لا يكلفونك طلبهم في بر ولا بحر, ولا جبل ولا سهل, الا انه طلب يسوءك وجدانه, وزور لا يسرك لقيانه, والسلام لاهله.
en: As regards your request to hand over to you the murderers of ‘Uthmān, I have thought over this matter and I do not find their handing over to you or to someone else possible for me. By my life, if you do not give up your wrong ways and disruptive acts you will surely know them. They will shortly be seeking you and will not give you the trouble of seeking them in land, sea, mountains or plain. But this search will be painful for you and their visit will not give you happiness. Peace be on those who deserve it.

t: Wisdom, friendship, and forbearance virtues
ar: ٦. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): صدر العاقل صندوق سره والبشاشه حباله الموده والاحتمال قبر العيوب‏.
en: 6. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The bosom of the wise is the safe of his secrets; cheerfulness is the bond of friendship; effective forbearance is the grave of short-comings.

t: Wisdom, friendship, and forbearance virtues
ar: وروي انه قال في العباره عن هذا المعني ايضا : المساله خباء العيوب ومن رضي عن نفسه كثر الساخط عليه.
en: It is narrated that Amīr al-mu’minīn said in expressing this meaning that: Mutual reconciliation is the covering for short-comings; and he who admires himself attracts many opponents against him.

t: Dyeing hair in early versus established Islam
ar: ١٧. وسيل (عليه ‏السلام) عن قول الرسول (صلي الله عليه واله وسلم): غيروا الشيب ولا تشبهوا باليهود. فقال (عليه ‏السلام):
en: 17. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, was asked to explain the saying of the Messenger of Allāh: “Banish your old age (by hair-dye) and do not acquire resemblance to the Jews.” Amīr al-mu’minīn replied:

t: Dyeing hair in early versus established Islam
ar: انما قال (صلي الله عليه واله وسلم) ذلك والدين قل فاما الان وقد اتسع نطاقه وضرب بجرانه فامرو وما اختار.
en: The Prophet (S) said this at a time when the religion was confined to a few, but now that its expanse has widened and it is firmly settled everyone is free in his action.

t: Self-reliance leads to ruin, consultation fosters wisdom
ar: ١٦١. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من استبد برايه هلك, ومن شاور الرجال شاركها في عقولها.
en: 161. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: He who acts solely according to his own opinion gets ruined, and he who consults other people shares in their understanding.

t: Destitution as the ultimate death
ar: ١٦٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الفقر الموت الاكبر.
en: 163. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Destitution is the greatest death.

t: Fulfilling rights of the ungrateful as idolatry
ar: ١٦٤. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من قضي حق من لا يقضي حقه فقد عبده.
en: 164. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: He who fulfils the right of a man who does not fulfil his right, (is as though he) worships him.

t: Blaming rightful claimants versus unjust takers
ar: ١٦٦. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): لا يعاب المرء بتاخير حقه, انما يعاب من اخذ ما ليس له.
en: 166. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: No person is to be blamed for delay in (securing) his own right but blame lies on him who takes what he is not entitled to.

t: Vanity hinders progress
ar: ١٦٧. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الاعجاب يمنع الازدياد.
en: 167. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Vanity prevents progress.

t: Divine guidance illuminates the perceptive
ar: ١٦٩. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): قد اضاء الصبح لذي عينين.
en: 169. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: For the man who has eyes the dawn has already appeared.

t: Avoiding sin is easier than seeking forgiveness
ar: ١٧٠. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ترك الذنب اهون من طلب التوبه.
en: 170. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Abstention from sin is easier than seeking forgiveness afterwards.

t: People oppose the unknown
ar: ١٧٢. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الناس اعداء ما جهلوا.
en: 172. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: People are enemies of what they do not know.

t: Multiple opinions reveal pitfalls
ar: ١٧٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من استقبل وجوه الاراء عرف مواقع الخطا.
en: 173. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: He who has several opinions understands the pitfalls.

t: Divine empowerment through righteous anger
ar: ١٧٤. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من احد سنان الغضب لله قوي علي قتل اشداء الباطل.
en: 174. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: He who sharpens the teeth of anger for the sake of Allāh acquires the strength to kill the stalwarts of wrong.

t: Abandoning right without supporting wrong
ar: ١٨. وقال (عليه ‏السلام) في الذين اعتزلوا القتال معه: خذلوا الحق, ولم ينصروا الباطل.
en: 18. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said about those who avoided fighting on his side: They abandoned right but did not support wrong.

t: Generosity as the foundation of leadership
ar: ١٧٦. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اله الرياسه سعه الصدر
en: 176. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The means to secure high authority is breadth of chest (i.e., generosity).

t: Rewarding the good to rebuke evil
ar: ١٧٧. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ازجر المسيء بثواب المحسن.
en: 177. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Rebuke the evil-doer by rewarding the good-doer.

t: Stubbornness undermines counsel
ar: ١٧٩. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اللجاجه تسل الراي.
en: 179. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Stubbornness destroys (good) advice.

t: Greed as perpetual enslavement
ar: ١٨٠. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الطمع رق موبد.
en: 180. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him said: Greed is a lasting slavery.

t: Silence in wisdom versus speech in folly
ar: ١٨٢. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): لا خير في الصمت عن الحكم, كما انه لا خير في القول بالجهل.
en: 182. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: There is no advantage ill keeping quiet about an issue of wisdom, just as there is no good in speaking out an unintelligent thing.

t: Two calls signify one leads to misguidance
ar: ١٨٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ما اختلفت دعوتان الا كانت احداهما ضلاله.
en: 183. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: If there are two different calls then one (of them) must be towards misguidance.

t: Unwavering certainty in recognizing truth
ar: ١٨٤. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ما شككت في الحق مذ اريته.
en: 184. Amir al-mu'minin, peace be upon him, said: I have never entertained doubt about right since I was shown it.

t: Oppression leads to inevitable regret
ar: ١٨٦. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): للظالم البادي غدا بكفه عضه.
en: 186. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: He who takes the lead in oppression has to bite his hand (in repentance) tomorrow.

t: Imminent departure from this world
ar: ١٨٧. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الرحيل وشيك.
en: 187. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The departure (from this world) is imminent.

t: Patience as relief versus impatience as destruction
ar: ١٨٩. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من لم ينجه الصبر اهلكه الجزع.
en: 189. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: If patience does not give relief to a man impatience kills him.

t: Forgiving virtuous individuals' shortcomings
ar: ٢٠. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اقيلوا ذوي المروءات عثراتهم فما يعثر منهم عاثر الا ويده بيد الله يرفعه.
en: 20. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Forgive the shortcomings of people of virtue and honor because when they fall into error Allāh raises them up.

t: Human mortality and life's fleeting nature
ar: ١٩١. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): انما المرء في الدنيا غرض تنتضل فيه المنايا ونهب تبادره المصايب ومع كل جرعه شرق وفي كل اكله غصص ولا ينال العبد نعمه الا بفراق اخري ولا يستقبل يوما من عمره الا بفراق اخر من اجله فنحن اعوان المنون وانفسنا نصب الحتوف فمن اين نرجو البقاء وهذا الليل والنهار لم يرفعا من شي‏ء شرفا الا اسرعا الكره في هدم ما بنيا و تفريق ما جمعا.
en: 191. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: In this world man is the target towards which the arrows of death fly, and is like that wealth whose destruction is quickened by hard ships. (In this world) with every drink there is suffocation and with every morsel there is choking. Here no one gets anything unless he loses something else, and not a day of his age advances till a day passes out from his life. Thus, we are helpers of death and our lives are the targets of mortality. How then can we expect everlasting life since the night and day do not raise anything high without quickly arranging for the destruction of whatever they have built and for the splitting asunder of whatever they have joined together.

t: Wealth as stewardship beyond basic needs
ar: ١٩٢. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): يا ابن ادم ما كسبت فوق قوتك, فانت فيه خازن لغيرك.
en: 192. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: O son of Adam, whatever you earn beyond your basic needs you are only a treasurer of it for others.

t: Hearts act best when passionate and willing
ar: ١٩٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ان للقلوب شهوه واقبالا وادبارا, فاتوها من قبل شهوتها واقبالها, فان القلب اذا اكره عمي.
en: 193. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Hearts are imbued with passion and the power of advancing and retreating. Therefore, approach them for action at the time of their passionateness and when they are in a mood for advancing, because if hearts are forced (to do a thing) they will be blinded.

t: Misers' niggardly disputes over worthless possessions
ar: ١٩٥. وقال (عليه ‏السلام) وقد مر بقذر علي مزبله: هذا ما بخل به الباخلون.
en: 195. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, passed beside a dump of rubbish full of filth and remarked: This is what the misers used to be niggardly about.

t: Misers' niggardly disputes over worthless possessions
ar: وروي في خبر اخر انه قال: هذا ما كنتم تتنافسون فيه بالامس!
en: In another tradition it is related that he said: This is what you used to dispute with each other about until yesterday!

t: Hearts tire like bodies; seek refreshing wisdom
ar: ١٩٧. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ان هذه القلوب تمل كما تمل الابدان, فابتغوا لها طرايف الحكمه.
en: 197. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The hearts become tired as the bodies become tired. You should therefore search for beautiful sayings for them (to enjoy by way of refreshment).

t: Khārijites' misinterpretation of divine verdict
ar: ١٩٨. وقال (عليه ‏السلام) لما سمع قول الخوارج لا حكم الا لله : كلمه حق يراد بها باطل.
en: 198. When Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, heard the slogan of the Khārijites: There is no verdict save of Allāh, he said: This sentence is true but it is interpreted wrongly.

t: Amīr al-mu’minīn rebukes the crowd for appearing only in disgraceful situations
ar: ٢٠٠. وقال (عليه ‏السلام) وقد اتي بجان ومعه غوغاء: فقال : لا مرحبا بوجوه لا تري الا عند كل سواه .
en: 200. An offender was brought before Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, and there was a crowd of people with the man, so Amīr al-mu’minīn remarked: Woe to the faces who are seen only on foul occasions.

t: Talḥah and Zubayr's conditional allegiance rejected by ʿAlī
ar: ٢٠٢. وقال (عليه ‏السلام) وقد قال له طلحه والزبير: نبايعك علي انا شركاوك في هذا الامر: لا, ولكنكما شريكان في القوه والاستعانه, وعونان علي العجز والاود.
en: 202. When Ṭalḥah and az-Zubayr said to him: We are prepared to swear allegiance to you on condition that we have a share with you in this matter (of caliphate), Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: No, but you will have a share in strengthening (the caliphate) and in affording assistance and you will both be helping me at the time of need and hardship.

t: Knowledge expands unlike physical containers
ar: ٢٠٥. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): كل وعاء يضيق بما جعل فيه الا وعاء العلم, فانه يتسع به.
en: 205. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Every container gets narrower according to what is placed in it except the container of knowledge which expands instead.

t: Forbearance earns allies against ignorance
ar: ٢٠٦. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اول عوض الحليم من حلمه ان الناس انصاره علي الجاهل.
en: 206. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The first reward the exerciser of forbearance gets is that people become his helpers against the ignorant.

t: Consequences of fear and missed opportunities
ar: ٢١. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): قرنت الهيبه بالخيبه والحياء بالحرمان والفرصه تمر مر السحاب, فانتهزوا فرص الخير.
en: 21. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The consequence of fear is disappointment and of bashfulness is frustration. Opportunity passes away like the cloud. Therefore, make use of good opportunities.

t: Self-reflection benefits, neglect harms; fear ensures safety, observation yields wisdom
ar: ٢٠٨. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من حاسب نفسه ربح, ومن غفل عنها خسر, ومن خاف امن, ومن اعتبر ابصر ومن ابصر فهم ومن فهم علم.
en: 208. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Whoever takes account of his self is benefited, and whoever remains neglectful of it suffers. Whoever fears remains safe; whoever takes instruction (from things around) perceives; and whoever perceives gains understanding, and whoever gains understanding secures knowledge.

t: Fear Allah with preparation and urgency
ar: ٢١٠. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اتقوا الله تقيه من شمر تجريدا وجد تشميرا وكمش في مهل وبادر عن وجل ونظر في كره المويل وعاقبه المصدر ومغبه المرجع.
en: 210. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Fear Allāh like the one who prepares himself after extracting himself (from worldly affairs) and after getting ready in this way makes effort; then he acts quickly during the period of this life, hastens in view of the dangers (of falling into error) and has his eye on proceeding towards the goal, on the end of his journey and on the place of his (eventual) return.

t: Virtues and vices in leadership and conduct
ar: ٢١١. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الجود حارس الاعراض والحلم فدام السفيه والعفو زكاه الظفر والسلو عوضك ممن غدر والاستشاره عين الهدايه وقد خاطر من استغني برايه والصبر يناضل الحدثان والجزع من اعوان الزمان واشرف الغني ترك المني وكم من عقل اسير تحت هوي امير ومن التوفيق حفظ التجربه والموده قرابه مستفاده ولا تامنن ملولا.
en: 211. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Generosity is the protector of honour; forbearance is the bridle of the fool; forgiveness is the levy of success; disregard is the punishment of him who betrays; and consultation is the chief way of guidance. He who is content with his own opinion faces danger. Endurance braves calamities while impatience is a helper of the hardships of the world. The best contentment is to give up desires. Many a slavish mind is subservient to overpowering longings. Capability helps preservation of experience. Love means well-utilized relationship. Do not trust one who is grieved.

t: Ignoring pain for lasting happiness
ar: ٢١٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اغض علي القذي والالم ترض ابدا.
en: 213. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Ignore pain otherwise you will never be happy. [Or according to another reading): Ignore pain and grief; you will ever be happy.]

t: Soft-trunked trees bear thick branches
ar: ٢١٤. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من لان عوده كثفت اغصانه.
en: 214. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The tree whose trunk is soft has thick branches.

t: Generosity leads to honor or corruption
ar: ٢١٦. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من نال استطال.
en: 216. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: He who gives generously achieves position. (Or according to another interpretation): He who achieves position begins to make wrong use of it.

t: Revealing character through changing circumstances
ar: ٢١٧. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): في تقلب الاحوال علم جواهر الرجال.
en: 217. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Through change of circumstances the mettle of men is known.

t: Intellectual deficiency caused by greed
ar: ٢١٩. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اكثر مصارع العقول تحت بروق المطامع.
en: 219. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Most of the deficiency of intelligence occurs due to the flash of greed.

t: Justice requires certainty, not probability
ar: ٢٢٠. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ليس من العدل القضاء علي الثقه بالظن.
en: 220. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: There is no justice in passing a verdict by relying on probability.

t: Noble restraint in overlooking known faults
ar: ٢٢٢. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من اشرف افعال الكريم غفلته عما يعلم.
en: 222. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The highest act of a noble person is to ignore what he knows.

t: Deeds outweigh lineage in merit
ar: ٢٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من ابطا به عمله لم يسرع به حسبه.
en: 23. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: He whose deeds accord (him) a back position cannot be given a front position because of his lineage.

t: Modesty conceals human flaws
ar: ٢٢٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من كساه الحياء ثوبه لم ير الناس عيبه.
en: 223. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Whomever modesty clothes with its dress people cannot see his defects.

t: Jealousy overlooks bodily health
ar: ٢٢٥. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): العجب لغفله الحساد عن سلامه الاجساد.
en: 225. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: It is strange that the jealous do not feel jealous about bodily health.

t: Greed shackles with disgrace
ar: ٢٢٦. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الطامع في وثاق الذل.
en: 226. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The greedy is in the shackles of disgrace.

t: Displeasure with divine decree and worldly attachment
ar: ٢٢٨. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من اصبح علي الدنيا حزينا فقد اصبح لقضاء الله ساخطا, ومن اصبح يشكو مصيبه نزلت به فقد اصبح يشكو ربه, ومن اتي غنيا فتواضع لغناه ذهب ثلثا دينه, ومن قرا القران فمات فدخل النار فهو ممن كان يتخذ ايات الله هزوا, ومن لهج قلبه بحب الدنيا التاط قلبه منها بثلاث: هم لا يغبه, وحرص لا يتركه, وامل لا يدركه.
en: 228. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: He who is sorrowful for this world is in fact displeased with the dispensation of Allāh. He who complains of a calamity that befalls him complains of his Lord (Allāh). He who approaches a rich man and bends before him on account of his riches then two-third of his religion is gone. If a man reads the Qur’ān and on dying goes to Hell then it means that he was among those who treated Divine verses with mockery. If a man’s heart gets attached to the world, then it catches three things, namely worry that never leaves him, greed that does not abandon him and desire which he never fulfils.

t: The virtues of contentment and moral character
ar: ٢٢٩. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): كفي بالقناعه ملكا, وبحسن الخلق نعيما. وسيل (عليه السلام) عن قوله تعالي ﴿فلنحيينه حياه طيبه﴾ فقال: هي القناعه.
en: 229. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Contentment is as good as estate, and goodness of moral character is as good as a blessing. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, was asked about Allāh’s saying: We will certainly make him live a life good and pure. (Qur’ān, 16:97) when he said: that means contentment.

t: Divine Command for Justice and Benevolence
ar: ٢٣١. وقال (عليه ‏السلام) في قوله تعالي ﴿ان الله يامر بالعدل والاحسان﴾: العدل الانصاف, والاحسان التفضل.
en: 231. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said about Allāh’s saying: Verily, Allāh enjoins justice (‘adl) and benevolence (iḥsān),(Qur’ān,16:90). Here ‘adl means equidistribution and iḥsān means favour.

t: Divine reward for modest charity
ar: ٢٣٢. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من يعط باليد القصيره يعط باليد الطويله.
en: 232. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: He who gives with his short hand is given by a long hand.

t: Divine reward for modest charity
ar: قال السيد الرضي: ومعني ذلك ان ما ينفقه المرء من ماله في سبيل الخير و البر و ان كان يسيرا فان الله تعالي يجعل الجزاء عليه عظيما كثيرا و اليدان هاهنا عباره عن النعمتين ففرق (عليه السلام) بين نعمه العبد و نعمه الرب تعالي ذكره بالقصيره و الطويله فجعل تلك قصيره و هذه طويله لان نعم الله ابدا تضعف علي نعم المخلوق اضعافا كثيره اذ كانت نعم الله اصل النعم كلها فكل نعمه اليها ترجع و منها تنزع .
en: as-Sayyid ar-Raḍī says: The meaning of this saying is that even though what a man spends in charity from his possessions may be small, yet Allāh, the Sublime, gives good reward for it. And the two hands referred to, here means two favours. Thus, Amīr al-mu’minīn has differentiated between the favour of man and the favour of the Lord (Allāh) — exalted be the mention of His name — since he has described the first as small and the other as big. This is because the favours of Allāh are ever multiplied manifold to the favours of man since Allāh’s favours are basic in the sense that every other favour springs from it and turns to it.

t: Gender-specific virtues in traditional Islamic ethics
ar: ٢٣٤. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): خيار خصال النساء شرار خصال الرجال: الزهو والجبن والبخل, فاذا كانت المراه مزهوه لم تمكن من نفسها, واذا كانت بخيله حفظت مالها ومال بعلها, واذا كانت جبانه فرقت من كل شيء يعرض لها.
en: 234. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The best traits of women are those which are the worst traits of men, namely: vanity, cowardice and miserliness. Thus, since the woman is vain, she will not allow anyone access to herself; since she is miserly, she will preserve her own property and the property of her husband; and since she is weak-hearted, she will be frightened with everything that befalls her.

t: Wisdom and ignorance in proper placement
ar: ٢٣٥. وقيل له (عليه السلام): صف لنا العاقل. فقال (عليه السلام): هو الذي يضع الشيء مواضعه. فقيل: فصف لنا الجاهل. فقال: قد فعلت.
en: 235. It was said to Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him: Describe the wise to us; and he said: The wise is one who places things in their proper positions. Then, he was asked: Describe the ignorant to us; and he said: I have already done so.

t: Wisdom and ignorance in proper placement
ar: قال السيد الرضي: يعني ان الجاهل هو الذي لا يضع الشي‏ء مواضعه فكان ترك صفته صفه له اذ كان بخلاف وصف العاقل.
en: as-Sayyid ar-Raḍī says: The meaning is that the ignorant is one who does not place things in their proper positions. In this way, Amīr al-mu’minīn’s abstention from describing was a way of describing him because his attributes are just the opposite of the wise.

t: Motives for worship: desire, fear, and gratitude
ar: ٢٣٧. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ان قوما عبدوا الله رغبه فتلك عباده التجار, وان قوما عبدوا الله رهبه فتلك عباده العبيد, وان قوما عبدوا الله شكرا فتلك عباده الاحرار.
en: 237. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: A group of people worshipped Allāh out of desire for reward surely, this is the worship of traders. Another group worshipped Allāh out of fear, this is the worship of slaves. Still another group worshipped Allāh out of gratefulness, this is the worship of free men.

t: Relief and comfort as atonement for sins
ar: ٢٤. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من كفارات الذنوب العظام اغاثه الملهوف, والتنفيس عن المكروب.
en: 24. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Rendering relief to the grief-stricken and providing comfort in hardship are among the means of atonement for great sins.

t: Sluggards lose rights and backbiters lose friends
ar: ٢٣٩. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من اطاع التواني ضيع الحقوق, ومن اطاع الواشي ضيع الصديق.
en: 239. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: He who is a sluggard loses his rights and he who believes in the backbiter loses his friend.

t: Ill-gotten gains lead to ruin
ar: ٢٤٠. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الحجر الغصيب في الدار رهن علي خرابها.
en: 240. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: One ill-gotten piece of stone in a house is a guarantee for its ruin.

t: Ill-gotten gains lead to ruin
ar: قال السيد الرضي: و يروي هذا الكلام عن النبي (ص) و لا عجب ان يشتبه الكلامان لان مستقاهما من قليب و مفرغهما من ذنوب .
en: as-Sayyid ar-Raḍī says: In one tradition this saying is attributed to the Prophet. It is no wonder that the two sayings should resemble each other because they are driven from the same source and dispersed through the same means.

t: Fear Allah with even minimal devotion
ar: ٢٤٢. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اتق الله بعض التقي وان قل, واجعل بينك وبين الله سترا وان رق.
en: 242. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Fear Allāh to some degree (even) though it be little; and set a curtain between you and Allāh (even) though it be thin.

t: Numerous replies obscure correct points
ar: ٢٤٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اذا ازدحم الجواب خفي الصواب.
en: 243. Amīr al-mu’mnīn, peace be upon him, said: When replies are numerous the correct point remains obscure.

t: Capability diminishes desire
ar: ٢٤٥. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اذا كثرت المقدره قلت الشهوه.
en: 245. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: When capability increases, desire decreases.

t: Vigilance in preserving blessings
ar: ٢٤٦. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): احذروا نفار النعم فما كل شارد بمردود.
en: 246. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Keep on guard against the slipping away of blessings because not everything that runs away comes back.

t: Generosity surpasses kinship in prompting virtue
ar: ٢٤٧. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الكرم اعطف من الرحم.
en: 247. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Generosity is more prompting to good than regard for kinship.

t: Virtue in overcoming reluctance
ar: ٢٤٩. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): افضل الاعمال ما اكرهت نفسك عليه.
en: 249. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The best act is that which you have to force yourself to do.

t: Understanding Allah through human frailty
ar: ٢٥٠. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): عرفت الله سبحانه بفسخ العزايم وحل العقود.
en: 250. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: I came to know Allāh, the Glorified, through the breaking of determinations, change of intentions and losing of courage.

t: Divine commandments and their purposes in Islam
ar: ٢٥١. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): فرض الله الايمان تطهيرا من الشرك والصلاه تنزيها عن الكبر والزكاه تسبيبا للرزق والصيام ابتلاء لاخلاص الخلق والحج تقربه للدين والجهاد عزا للاسلام والامر بالمعروف مصلحه للعوام والنهي عن المنكر ردعا للسفهاء وصله الرحم منماه للعدد والقصاص حقنا للدماء واقامه الحدود اعظاما للمحارم وترك شرب الخمر تحصينا للعقل ومجانبه السرقه ايجابا للعفه وترك الزني تحصينا للنسب وترك اللواط تكثيرا للنسل والشهادات استظهارا علي المجاحدات وترك الكذب تشريفا للصدق والسلام امانا من المخاوف والامانه نظاما للامه والطاعه تعظيما للامامه.
en: 252. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Allāh has laid down īmān (belief) for purification from polytheism; ṣalāt (prayer) for purification from vanity; zakāt (levy) as a means of livelihood; ṣiyām (fasting) as a trial of the people; ḥajj (pilgrimage to the House of Allāh in Mecca) as a support for religion; jihād (fighting in the way of Allāh) for the honour of Islam; persuasion for good (al-amr bi’l-ma‘rūf ) for the good of the common people; dissuasion from evil (an-nahy ‘ani’l-munkar) for the control of the mischievous; regard for kinship for increase of number; revenge for stoppage of bloodshed; the award of penalties for the realization of importance of the prohibitions; the abstinence from drinking wine for protection of the wit; the avoidance of theft for inculcating chastity; abstinence from adultery for safeguarding descent; abstinence from sodomy for increase of progeny; tendering evidence for furnishing proof against contentions; abstinence from the lie for increasing esteem for truth; maintenance of peace (salām) for protection from danger; imāmah or Imāmate (Divine Leadership) for the orderliness of the community and obedience (to Imāms) as a mark of respect to the Imāmate.

t: Unintentional revelation of hidden thoughts
ar: ٢٦. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ما اضمر احد شييا الا ظهر في فلتات لسانه و صفحات وجهه.
en: 26. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Whenever a person conceals a thing in his heart it manifests itself through unintentional words from his tongue and (in) the expressions of his face.

t: Managing property as one's own representative
ar: ٢٥٤. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): يا ابن ادم كن وصي نفسك في مالك واعمل فيه ما توثر ان يعمل فيه من بعدك.
en: 254. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: O’ son of Adam, be your own representative in the matter of your property and do about it whatever you want to be done with it after your death.

t: Anger as a form of temporary madness
ar: ٢٥٥. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الحده ضرب من الجنون, لان صاحبها يندم, فان لم يندم فجنونه مستحكم.
en: 255. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Anger is a kind of madness because the victim to it repents afterwards. If he does not repent his madness is confirmed.

t: Health benefits of minimizing envy
ar: ٢٥٦. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): صحه الجسد من قله الحسد.
en: 256. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Health of body comes from paucity of envy.

t: Charitable trade with Allah in destitution
ar: ٢٥٨. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اذا املقتم فتاجروا الله بالصدقه.
en: 258. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: When you fall in destitution, trade with Allāh through charity.

t: Divine punishment through worldly indulgence
ar: ٢٦٠. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): كم من مستدرج بالاحسان اليه, ومغرور بالستر عليه, ومفتون بحسن القول فيه, وما ابتلي الله سبحانه احدا بمثل الاملاء له.
en: 260. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: There is many a man being gradually brought towards punishment by good treatment with him; many a man who remains in deceit because his evils are covered; and many a man who is in illusion because of good talk about him, while there is no greater ordeal by Allāh, the Glorified, than the giving of time.

t: Divine punishment through worldly indulgence
ar: قال السيد الرضي: وقد مضي هذا الكلام فيما تقدم, الا ان فيه هاهنا زياده مفيده
en: as-Sayyid ar-Raḍī says: This saying has appeared earlier as well but here it contains a beautiful and useful addition.

t: Ali's lament over his people's disobedience
ar: ٢٦١. وقال (عليه السلام) لما بلغه اغاره اصحاب معاويه علي الانبار, فخرج بنفسه ماشيا حتي اتي النخيله وادركه الناس وقالوا: يا امير المومنين نحن نكفيكهم. فقال (عليه السلام) :
en: 261. When the news of the attack of Mu‘āwiyah’s men on al-Anbār reached Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, he himself came out walking till he reached an-Nukhaylah, where people overtook him and said: “O’ Amīr al-mu’minīn, we are enough for them,” then he said:

t: Ali's lament over his people's disobedience
ar: والله ما تكفونني انفسكم, فكيف تكفونني غيركم? ان كانت الرعايا قبلي لتشكوا حيف رعاتها, وانني اليوم لاشكو حيف رعيتي, كانني المقود وهم القاده, او الموزوع وهم الوزعه.
en: You cannot be enough for me against yourselves, so how can you be enough for me against others? Before me the people used to complain of the oppression of their rulers but now I have to complain of the wrongful actions of my people; as though I am led by them and they are the leaders or that I am the subject and they are the rulers.

t: Ali's lament over his people's disobedience
ar: فلما قال (عليه السلام) هذا القول, في كلام طويل قد ذكرنا مختاره في جمله الخطب, تقدم اليه رجلان من اصحابه, فقال احدهما: اني لا املك الا نفسي واخي, فمرنا بامرك يا اميرالمومنين ننفد له. فقال (عليه السلام): واين تقعان مما اريد?
en: The narrator says: When Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, uttered this during his long speech which we have included in the collection of sermons (No. 27), two men from his companions advanced towards him and one of them said : I rule no one except myself and my brother (Qur’ān, 5:25). So, order us with your command, O’ Amīr al-mu’minīn and we will accomplish it. Thereupon, Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: How can you two accomplish what I aim at?

t: Discerning right from wrong in leadership
ar: ٢٦٢. وقيل: ان الحارث بن حوط اتاه (عليه السلام) فقال: ا ترانی اظن اصحاب الجمل كانوا علي ضلاله?
en: 262. It is said that al-Ḥārith ibn Ḥawṭ came to Amīr al-mu’minīn, and said: Do you believe I can ever imagine that the people of Jamal were in the wrong?

t: Discerning right from wrong in leadership
ar: فقال (عليه السلام) : يا حارث, انك نظرت تحتك ولم تنظر فوقك فحرت انك لم تعرف الحق فتعرف من اتاه, ولم تعرف الباطل فتعرف من اتاه.
en: Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: O’ al-Ḥārith! You have seen below yourself but not above yourself, and so you have been confused. Certainly, you have not known right, so that you can recognize the righteous. And you have not known wrong, so that you can recognize the people of wrong!

t: Discerning right from wrong in leadership
ar: فقال الحارث: فاني اعتزل مع سعيد بن مالك وعبد الله بن عمر.
en: Then al-Ḥārith said: In that case, I shall withdraw along with Sa‘d ibn Mālik and ‘Abdullāh ibn ‘Umar;

t: Discerning right from wrong in leadership
ar: فقال (عليه السلام) : ان سعيدا وعبد الله بن عمر لم ينصرا الحق, ولم يخذلا الباطل.
en: whereupon Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Verily, Sa‘d and ‘Umar have neither sided with Right nor forsaken Wrong.

t: Reciprocal kindness toward the bereaved
ar: ٢٦٤. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): احسنوا في عقب غيركم تحفظوا في عقبكم.
en: 264. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Do good with the bereaved ones of others so that good is done to your bereaved ones also.

t: Wise words as cure or illness
ar: ٢٦٥. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ان كلام الحكماء اذا كان صوابا كان دواء, واذا كان خطا كان داء.
en: 265. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: When the utterance of the wise is to the point it serves as a cure, but if it is wrong it proves like an illness.

t: Trusting divine provision for future days
ar: ٢٦٧. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): يابن ادم, لا تحمل هم يومك الذي لم ياتك علي يومك الذي قد اتاك, فانه ان يك من عمرك يات الله فيه برزقك.
en: 267. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: O’ son of Adam, do not inflict the worry of the day that has not yet come on the day which has already come, because if that day be in your life Allāh will bestow its livelihood also.

t: Walking during illness when possible
ar: ٢٧. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): امش بدايك ما مشي بك.
en: 27. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Keep walking in your sickness as long as you can.

t: Balanced love and hatred in relationships
ar: ٢٦٨. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): احبب حبيبك هونا ما عسي ان يكون بغيضك يوما ما, وابغض بغيضك هونا ما عسي ان يكون حبيبك يوما ما.
en: 268. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Have love for your friend up to a limit, for it is possible that he may turn into your enemy some day; and hate your enemy up to a limit for it is possible that he may turn into your friend some day.

t: Caliph 'Umar's deliberation on Ka'bah ornaments
ar: ٢٧٠. وروي انه ذكر عند عمر بن الخطاب في ايامه حلي الكعبه وكثرته, فقال قوم: لو اخذته فجهزت به جيوش المسلمين كان اعظم للاجر, وما تصنع الكعبه بالحلي? فهم عمر بذلك, وسال عنه امير المومنين (عليه السلام).
en: 270. It is related that during the days of (Caliph) ‘Umar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb, the question of the excess of the ornaments of the Ka‘bah was mentioned to him and some people suggested: If you prepare with it an army of Muslims that will be a matter of great reward; and what would the Ka‘bah do with the ornaments? ‘Umar thought of doing so but asked Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, when he said:

t: Caliph 'Umar's deliberation on Ka'bah ornaments
ar: فقال (عليه السلام): ان القران انزل علي النبي (صلي الله عليه واله) والاموال اربعه: اموال المسلمين فقسمها بين الورثه في الفرايض, والفيء فقسمه علي مستحقيه, والخمس فوضعه الله حيث وضعه, والصدقات فجعلها الله حيث جعلها. وكان حلي الكعبه فيها يوميذ, فتركه الله علي حاله, ولم يتركه نسيانا, ولم يخف عليه مكانا, فاقره حيث اقره الله ورسوله.
en: When the Qur’ān was descended on the Prophet, peace be upon him and his descendants, there were four kinds of property. One, the property of Muslim individuals which he distributed among the successors according to fixed shares. Second, the tax (fay’) which he distributed to those for whom it was meant. Third, the One-fifth (khums) levy for which Allāh had fixed the ways of disposal. Fourth, amounts of charity (ṣadaqāt) whose disposal was also fixed by Allāh. The ornaments of Ka‘bah did exist in those days but Allāh left them as they were, but did not leave them by omission, nor were they unknown to Him. Therefore, you retain them where Allāh and His Prophet placed them.

t: Caliph 'Umar's deliberation on Ka'bah ornaments
ar: فقال له عمر: لولاك لافتضحنا. وترك الحلي بحاله.
en: Thereupon, ‘Umar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb said: If you had not been here we would have been humiliated; and he left the ornaments as they were.

t: Amīr al-mu’minīn's resolve to enact changes
ar: ٢٧٢. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): لو قد استوت قدماي من هذه المداحض لغيرت اشياء.
en: 272. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: If my steps acquire firmness out of these slippery places, I will alter several things.

t: Divine decree and contentment in livelihood
ar: ٢٧٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اعلموا علما يقينا ان الله لم يجعل للعبد وان عظمت حيلته واشتدت طلبته و قويت مكيدته اكثر مما سمي له في الذكر الحكيم ولم يحل بين العبد في ضعفه وقله حيلته وبين ان يبلغ ما سمي له في الذكر الحكيم والعارف لهذا العامل به اعظم الناس راحه في منفعه والتارك له الشاك فيه اعظم الناس شغلا في مضره ورب منعم عليه مستدرج بالنعمي ورب مبتلي مصنوع له بالبلوي فزد ايها المستنفع في شكرك وقصر من عجلتك وقف عند منتهي رزقك.
en: 273. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Know with full conviction that Allāh has not fixed for any person more livelihood than what has been ordained in the Book of Destiny, even though his means (of seeking it) may be great, his craving for it intense and his efforts for it acute; nor does the weakness of a person or the paucity of his means stand in the way between what is ordained in the Book of Destiny and himself. He who realizes it and acts upon it is the best of them all in point of comfort and benefit; while he who disregards it and doubts it exceeds all men in disadvantages. Very often a favoured person is being slowly driven (towards punishment) through those favours; and very often an afflicted person is being done good through his affliction. Therefore, O’ listener, increase your gratefulness, lessen your haste and stay within the bounds of your livelihood.

t: The destructive consequences of greed and unfulfilled desires
ar: ٢٧٥. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ان الطمع مورد غير مصدر وضامن غير وفي وربما شرق شارب الماء قبل ريه وكلما عظم قدر الشي‏ء المتنافس فيه عظمت الرزيه لفقده والاماني تعمي اعين البصاير والحظ ياتي من لا ياتيه.
en: 275. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Greed takes a person to the watering place but gets him back without letting him drink. It undertakes responsibility but does not fulfil it. Often the drinker gets choked before the quenching of his thirst. The greater the worth of a thing yearned for the greater is the grief for its loss. Desires blind the eyes of understanding. The destined share will reach him who does not approach it.

t: Amīr al-mu’minīn's oath on divine truth
ar: ٢٧٧. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): لا والذي امسينا منه في غبر ليله دهماء تكشرعن يوم اغر ما كان كذا وكذا.
en: 277. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: I swear by Him Who let us pass the dark night after which there was a bright day that such and such  did not happen.

t: Consistent small actions outweigh sporadic large efforts
ar: ٢٧٨. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): قليل تدوم عليه ارجي من كثير مملول منه.
en: 278. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: A small action which is continued with regularity is more beneficial than a long one performed with disgust.

t: Preparing for life's journey by anticipating distance
ar: ٢٨٠. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من تذكر بعد السفر استعد.
en: 280. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Whoever keeps in view the distance of the journey remains prepared.

t: True observation through wisdom, not eyes
ar: ٢٨١. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ليست الرويه كالمعاينه مع الابصار, فقد تكذب العيون اهلها, ولا يغش العقل من استنصحه.
en: 281. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Perception by the eyes is not real observation because the eyes sometimes deceive people; but wisdom does not deceive whomsoever it counsels.

t: Ignorance rewarded over knowledge
ar: ٢٨٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): جاهلكم مزداد وعالمكم مسوف.
en: 283. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The ignorant among you get too much while the learned are just put off.

t: Fleeing worldly life as death approaches
ar: ٢٩. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اذا كنت في ادبار والموت في اقبال فما اسرع الملتقي.
en: 29. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: When you are running away from the world and death is approaching, there is no question of delay in the encounter.

t: Knowledge dispels excuses
ar: ٢٨٤. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): قطع العلم عذر المتعللين.
en: 284. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Knowledge dispels the excuse of those who advance excuses.

t: Hidden evils in apparent goodness
ar: ٢٨٦. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ما قال الناس لشيء: طوبي له, الا وقد خبا له الدهر يوم سوء.
en: 286. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: For everything to which people say “how good!” there is an evil hidden in this world.

t: Destiny as a divine mystery beyond human comprehension
ar: ٢٨٧. وسيل عن القدر, فقال: طريق مظلم فلا تسلكوه, وبحرعميق فلا تلجوه, وسر الله فلا تتكلفوه.
en: 287. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, was asked about Destiny, when he said: It is a dark path — do not tread upon it, it is a deep ocean — do not dive in it, and it is the secret of Allāh — do not take trouble about (knowing) it.

t: Exemplary virtues of a devout brother-in-faith
ar: ٢٨٩. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): كان لي فيما مضي اخ في الله وكان يعظمه في عيني صغر الدنيا في عينه وكان خارجا من سلطان بطنه فلا يشتهي ما لا يجد ولا يكثر اذا وجد وكان اكثر دهره صامتا فان قال بذ القايلين ونقع غليل السايلين وكان ضعيفا مستضعفا فان جاء الجد فهو ليث غاب وصل واد.
en: 289. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: In the past I had a brother-in-faith , and he was prestigious in my view because the world was humble in his eyes, the needs of the stomach did not have sway over him, he did not long for what he did not get; if he got a thing he would not ask for more; most of his time he was silent, if he spoke he silenced the other speakers, he quenched the thirst of questioners, he was weak and feeble but at the time of fighting he was like the lion of the forest or the serpent of the valley.

t: Exemplary virtues of a devout brother-in-faith
ar: لا يدلي بحجه حتي ياتي قاضيا وكان لا يلوم احدا علي ما يجد العذر في مثله حتي يسمع اعتذاره وكان لا يشكو وجعا الا عند بريه وكان يقول ما يفعل ولا يقول ما لا يفعل وكان اذا غلب علي الكلام لم يغلب علي السكوت وكان علي ما يسمع احرص منه علي ان يتكلم وكان اذا بدهه امران ينظر ايهما اقرب الي الهوي فيخالفه.
en: He would not put forth an argument unless it was decisive, he would not abuse anyone in an excusable matter unless he had heard the excuse, he would not speak of any trouble except after its disappearance, he would say what he would do, and would not say what he would not do, even if he could be exceeded in speaking, he could not be excelled in silence, he was more eager for keeping quiet than speaking and if two things confronted him he would see which was more akin to the longing of the heart and he would oppose it.

t: Exemplary virtues of a devout brother-in-faith
ar: فعليكم بهذه الخلايق فالزموها وتنافسوا فيها فان لم تستطيعوها فاعلموا ان اخذ القليل خير من ترك الكثير.
en: These qualities are incumbent upon you. So, you should acquire them and excel each other in them. Even if you cannot acquire them you should know that acquiring a part is better than giving up the whole.

t: Divine obedience through gratitude for God's favors
ar: ٢٩٠. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): لو لم يتوعد الله علي معصيه لكان يجب ان لا يعصي شكرا لنعمه.
en: 290. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Even if Allāh had not warned of chastisement on those disobedient to Him, it would be obligatory by way of gratefulness for His favours that He should not be disobeyed.

t: Amir al-mu'minin's lamentation for the Prophet's passing
ar: ٢٩٢. وقال (عليه السلام): علي قبر رسول الله (صلي الله عليه واله) ساعه دفنه:
en: 292. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said on the grave of the Messenger of Allāh, peace be upon him and his descendants, at the time of burial:

t: Amir al-mu'minin's lamentation for the Prophet's passing
ar: ان الصبر لجميل الا عنك, وان الجزع لقبيح الا عليك, وان المصاب بك لجليل, وانه قبلك وبعدك لجلل.
en: Certainly, endurance is good except about you; fretting is bad except over you; and the affliction about you is great while every other affliction before or after it is small.

t: Avoiding association with fools
ar: ٢٩٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): لا تصحب المايق فانه يزين لك فعله, ويود ان تكون مثله.
en: 293. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Do not associate with a fool because he will beautify his actions before you and long that you too be like him.

t: Three types of friends and enemies
ar: ٢٩٥. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اصدقاوك ثلاثه, واعداوك ثلاثه: فاصدقاوك: صديقك, وصديق صديقك, وعدو عدوك. واعداوك: عدوك, وعدو صديقك, وصديق عدوك.
en: 295. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Your friends are three and your enemies are (also) three. Your friends are: your friend, your friend’s friend and your enemy’s enemy. And your enemies are: your enemy, your friend’s enemy and your enemy’s friend.

t: Self-harm in pursuit of harming others
ar: ٢٩٦. وقال (عليه ‏السلام) لرجل راه يسعي علي عدو له بما فيه اضرار بنفسه: انما انت كالطاعن نفسه ليقتل ردفه.
en: 296. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, saw a man busy against his enemy with what was harmful to himself too, so he said: You are like one who pierces a spear through himself in order to kill the person sitting behind him.

t: Excessive quarreling leads to sin while deficiency invites oppression
ar: ٢٩٨. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من بالغ في الخصومه اثم, ومن قصر فيها ظلم, ولا يستطيع ان يتقي الله من خاصم.
en: 298. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: He who goes too far in quarrelling is a sinner, but if one falls short in it, one is oppressed and it is difficult for a quarreller to fear Allāh.

t: Divine concealment of sins as a mercy
ar: ٣٠. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الحذر! الحذر! فوالله لقد ستر, حتي كانه قد غفر.
en: 30. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Fear! Fear! By Allāh, He has hidden your sins so much so as though He has forgiven.

t: Divine sustenance and accountability parallels
ar: ٣٠٠. وسيل (عليه السلام) : كيف يحاسب الله الخلق علي كثرتهم?
en: 300. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, was asked: How Allāh would conduct the accounting of all persons despite their large number.

t: Divine sustenance and accountability parallels
ar: فقال (عليه السلام) : كما يرزقهم علي كثرتهم.
en: He replied: Just as He provides them livelihood despite their large number.

t: Divine sustenance and accountability parallels
ar: فقيل: كيف يحاسبهم ولا يرونه?
en: Then it was said to him: How will He conduct their accounting without their seeing Him.

t: Divine sustenance and accountability parallels
ar: فقال (عليه السلام) : كما يرزقهم ولا يرونه.
en: He replied: Just as He provides them livelihood although they do not see Him.

t: Hardship's inevitability and prayer's necessity
ar: ٣٠٢. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ما المبتلي الذي قد اشتد به البلاء, باحوج الي الدعاء من المعافي الذي لا يامن البلاء.
en: 302. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The person who is afflicted with hardship is not in greater need of praying than the one who has been spared affliction but is not immune from it.

t: People's love for the world as their mother
ar: ٣٠٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الناس ابناء الدنيا, ولا يلام الرجل علي حب امه.
en: 303. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: People are the progeny of the world and no one can be blamed for loving the mother.

t: Destitute as the Messenger of Allah
ar: ٣٠٤. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ان المسكين رسول الله, فمن منعه فقد منع الله, ومن اعطاه فقد اعطي الله.
en: 304. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The destitute is the Messenger of Allāh. Whoever denies him denies Allāh and whoever gives him gives Allāh.

t: Life's brevity necessitates vigilance
ar: ٣٠٦. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): كفي بالاجل حارسا.
en: 306. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The fixed limit of life is enough to remain watchful.

t: Patience in grief versus material loss
ar: ٣٠٧. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ينام الرجل علي الثكل ولا ينام علي الحرب.
en: 307. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: A man can sleep on the death of his child, but cannot sleep at loss of property.

t: Patience in grief versus material loss
ar: قال السيد الرضي: ومعني ذلك: انه يصبر علي قتل الاولاد, ولا يصبر علي سلب الاموال.
en: as-Sayyid ar-Raḍī says: It means that a man keeps patience on the death of his children but does not do so on the loss of property.

t: Believers' insights reflect divine truth
ar: ٣٠٩. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اتقوا ظنون المومنين, فان الله تعالي جعل الحق علي السنتهم.
en: 309. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Be afraid of the ideas of believers, because Allāh, the Sublime, has put truth on their tongues.

t: Balancing obligatory and optional acts in fluctuating devotion
ar: ٣١٢. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ان للقلوب اقبالا وادبارا فاذا اقبلت فاحملوها علي النوافل, واذا ادبرت فاقتصروا بها علي الفرايض.
en: 312. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Sometimes the hearts move forward and sometimes they move backward. When they move forward get them to perform the optionals (as well), but when they move backward keep them confined to obligatories only.

t: Qur'an's past, future, and present guidance
ar: ٣١٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): وفي القران نبا ما قبلكم, وخبر ما بعدكم, وحكم ما بينكم.
en: 313. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The Qur’ān contains news about the past, foretellings about the future and commandments for the present.

t: Calligraphy and writing aesthetics in Islamic tradition
ar: ٣١٥. وقال (عليه ‏السلام) لكاتبه عبيد الله بن ابي رافع: الق دواتك واطل جلفه قلمك وفرج بين السطور وقرمط بين الحروف فان ذلك اجدر بصباحه الخط.
en: 315. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said to his secretary ‘Ubaydullāh ibn Abī Rāfi‘: Put cotton flake in the ink-pot, keep the nib of your pen long, leave space between lines and close up the letters because this is good for the beauty of the writing.

t: Charity as a cure and afterlife consequences
ar: ٧. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الصدقه دواء منجح, واعمال العباد في عاجلهم, نصب اعينهم في اجلهم.
en: 7. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Charity is an effective cure, and the actions of people in their present life will be before their eyes in the next life.

t: The doer of good surpasses the good itself
ar: ٣٢. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): فاعل الخير خير منه, وفاعل الشر شر منه.
en: 32. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The doer of good is better than the good itself, and the doer of evil is worse than the evil itself.

t: Early disputes over Prophet Muhammad's succession
ar: ٣١٧. وقال له بعض اليهود: ما دفنتم نبيكم حتي اختلفتم فيه. فقال (عليه السلام) له: انما اختلفنا عنه لا فيه ولكنكم ما جفت ارجلكم من البحر حتي قلتم لنبيكم ﴿اجعل لنا الها كما لهم الهه ۚ قال انكم قوم تجهلون﴾.
en: 317. Some Jews said to Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him: You had not buried your Prophet when you picked up differences about him, when Amīr al-mu’minīn replied: We did not differ about him but we differed after him (i.e. about his succession); whereas you had not dried up your feet after coming out of the river (Nile) when you began asking your Prophet: Make you for us a god as they have gods of their own. Said he: ‘Verily you are a people behaving ignorantly.’ (Qur’ān, 7:138)

t: Overcoming adversaries through awe-inspiring presence
ar: ٣١٨. وقيل له (عليه ‏السلام): باي شي‏ء غلبت الاقران, فقال (عليه السلام): ما لقيت رجلا الا اعانني علي نفسه.
en: 318. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, was asked: With what did you overpower your adversaries? He answered: Whenever I confronted a person he helped me against himself.

t: Overcoming adversaries through awe-inspiring presence
ar: قال السيد الرضي: يومي بذلك الي تمكن هيبته في القلوب.
en: as-Sayyid ar-Raḍī says: Amīr al-mu’minīn is pointing out his striking of awe in the hearts.

t: Seeking understanding over confusion from learned guidance
ar: ٣٢٠. وقال (عليه ‏السلام) لسايل ساله عن معضله: سل تفقها ولا تسال تعنتا فان الجاهل المتعلم شبيه بالعالم وان العالم المتعسف شبيه بالجاهل المتعنت.
en: 320. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, replied to a man who had asked him a difficult question: Ask me for understanding but do not ask me for confusion, because the ignorant person who tries to learn is like the learned man, but the learned man who tries to create confusion is like the ignorant.

t: Amīr al-mu’minīn's encounter with Ḥarb ash-Shibāmi
ar: ٣٢٢. وروي انه (عليه السلام) لما ورد الكوفه قادما من صفين مر بالشباميين فسمع بكاء النساء علي قتلي صفين, وخرج اليه حرب بن شرحبيل الشبامي, وكان من وجوه قومه. فقال (عليه السلام): له ا تغلبكم نساوكم علي ما اسمع? ا لا تنهونهن عن هذا الرنين,
en: 322. When Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, returned to Kūfah from Ṣiffīn he passed by the residences of the Shibāmites (who belonged to the tribe of Shibām) and heard the women weeping over those killed in Ṣiffīn. At that time a Shibāmite, Ḥarb ibn Shuraḥbil ash-Shibāmi, who was one of the nobles of those people, came to him, and Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said to him: Do your women have control over you as regards the weeping that I hear? Do you not refrain them from this crying?

t: Amīr al-mu’minīn's encounter with Ḥarb ash-Shibāmi
ar: واقبل حرب يمشي معه, وهو (عليه السلام) راكب. فقال (عليه السلام) له: ارجع, فان مشي مثلك مع مثلي فتنه للوالي, ومذله للمومن.
en: Ḥarb began to walk with him while Amīr al-mu’minīn was on horseback, so Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said to him: Get back because the walking of a man like you with one like me is mischief for the ruler and disgrace for the believer.

t: Disobeying Allah in solitude invites divine judgment
ar: ٣٢٤. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اتقوا معاصي الله في الخلوات, فان الشاهد هو الحاكم.
en: 324. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Beware of disobeying Allāh in solitude, because the witness (of that situation) is also the judge.

t: Amīr al-mu’minīn's grief over Muḥammad ibn Abī Bakr's death
ar: ٣٢٥. وقال (عليه ‏السلام) لما بلغه قتل محمد بن ابي بكر: ان حزننا عليه علي قدر سرورهم به, الا انهم نقصوا بغيضا, ونقصنا حبيبا.
en: 325. When the news of killing of Muḥammad ibn Abī Bakr  reached Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, he said: Our grief over him is as great as their (i.e. the enemy’s) joy for it, except that they have lost an enemy and we have lost a friend.

t: Triumph through sin is true defeat
ar: ۳۲۷. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ما ظفر من ظفر الاثم به, والغالب بالشر مغلوب.
en: 327. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: He whom sin overpowers is not victorious, and he who secures victory by evil is (in fact) vanquished.

t: Avoiding the need for excuses surpasses truthful excuses
ar: ۳۲۹. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الاستغناء عن العذر اعز من الصدق به.
en: 329. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Not to be in need of putting forth an excuse is better than putting forth a true excuse.

t: Minimizing divine favor misuse
ar: ۳۳۰. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اقل ما يلزمكم لله الا تستعينوا بنعمه علي معاصيه.
en: 330. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The least right of Allāh on you is that you should not make use of His favours in committing His sins.

t: The sovereign as divine guardian on earth
ar: ۳۳۲. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): السلطان وزعه الله في ارضه.
en: 332. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The sovereign is the watchman of Allāh on earth.

t: Balancing generosity and thrift
ar: ٣٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): كن سمحا ولا تكن مبذرا, وكن مقدرا ولا تكن مقترا.
en: 33. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Be generous but not extravagant; be thrifty but not miserly.

t: Believer's virtues of humility and inner strength
ar: ٣٣٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام) في صفه المومن: المومن بشره في وجهه وحزنه في قلبه اوسع شي‏ء صدرا واذل شي‏ء نفسا يكره الرفعه ويشنا السمعه طويل غمه بعيد همه كثير صمته مشغول وقته شكور صبور مغمور بفكرته ضنين بخلته سهل الخليقه لين العريكه نفسه اصلب من الصلد وهو اذل من العبد.
en: 333. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said describing a believer: A believer has a cheerful face, a sorrowful heart, a very broad chest (full of generosity), and a very humble heart. He hates high position and dislikes renown. His grief is long, his courage is far-reaching, his silence is much and, his time is occupied. He is grateful, enduring, buried in his thoughts, sparing in his friendship (with others), of bright demeanour and of soft temperament. He is stronger than stone but humbler than a slave.

t: Hating desires upon foreseeing fate
ar: ۳۳۴. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): لوراي العبد الاجل ومسيره لابغض الامل وغروره.
en: 334. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: If a man happens to see the end of (his) life and his final fate, he will begin hating desires and their deception.

t: The freedom of the uncommitted giver
ar: ۳۳۶. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): المسوول حر حتي يعد.
en: 336. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The person who is approached with a request is free until he promises.

t: Prayer without effort is futile as bowless archery
ar: ٣٣٧. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الداعي بلا عمل كالرامي بلا وتر.
en: 337. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: He who prays but does not exert effort is like the one who shoots without a bow-string.

t: Power and decision-making interdependence
ar: ٣٣٩. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): صواب الراي بالدول: يقبل باقبالها ويذهب بذهابها.
en: 339. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Correctness of decision goes together with power. The one emerges with the other’s emergence and disappears when the other disappears.

t: Justice harsher for oppressors than oppression for victims
ar: ٣٤١. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): يوم العدل علي الظالم اشد من يوم الجور علي المظلوم!
en: 341. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The day of justice will be severer on the oppressor than the day of oppression on the oppressed.

t: Contentment as the greatest wealth
ar: ٣٤٢. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الغني الاكبر الياس عما في ايدي الناس.
en: 342. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The biggest wealth is that one should not have an eye on what others possess.

t: Accountability for words and deeds
ar: ٣٤٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الاقاويل محفوظه والسراير مبلوه و﴿كل نفس بما كسبت رهينه﴾ والناس منقوصون مدخولون الا من عصم الله: سايلهم متعنت ومجيبهم متكلف يكاد افضلهم رايا يرده عن فضل رايه الرضي والسخط, ويكاد اصلبهم عودا تنكوه اللحظه وتستحيله الكلمه الواحده.
en: 343. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Utterances are preserved and actions are to be tried. Every soul, for what it earned, is held in pledge! (Qur’ān, 74:38). People are to be made deficient (as regards their bodies) and meddled with (as regards their minds) except those whom Allāh protects. The questioner among them aims at confusing and the answerer creates hardship. It is possible that the man who has the best views among them will be deviated from the soundness of his thinking by pleasure or displeasure, and it is possible that a single glance may affect even the man with the best wisdom among them or a single expression may transform him.

t: Lack of sin access as chastity
ar: ٣٤٥. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من العصمه تعذر المعاصي.
en: 345. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Lack of access to sins is also a kind of chastity

t: Dignity eroded by begging
ar: ٣٤٦. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ماء وجهك جامد يقطره السوال, فانظر عند من تقطره.
en: 346. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The dignity of your face is solid but begging dissolves it away; therefore, look carefully before whom you dissolve it.

t: Quick speech invites unfounded criticism
ar: ٣٥. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من اسرع الي الناس بما يكرهون, قالوا فيه بما لا يعلمون.
en: 35. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: If someone is quick in saying about people what they dislike, they speak about him that about which they have no knowledge.

t: The gravity of sins underestimated by their doers
ar: ٣٤٨. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): اشد الذنوب ما استهان به صاحبه.
en: 348. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: The most serious sin is that which the doer considers light.

t: Self-reflection and contentment in Islamic teachings
ar: ٣٤٩. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من نظر في عيب نفسه اشتغل عن عيب غيره, ومن رضي برزق الله لم يحزن علي ما فاته, ومن سل سيف البغي قتل به, ومن كابد الامور عطب, ومن اقتحم اللجج غرق, ومن دخل مداخل السوء اتهم,
en: 349. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: He who sees his own shortcomings abstains from looking into other’s shortcomings. He who feels happy with the livelihood with which Allāh provides him does not grieve over what he misses. He who draws out the sword of revolt gets killed with it. He who strives without means perishes. He who enters the depths gets drowned. He who visits places of ill-repute receives blame.

t: Self-reflection and contentment in Islamic teachings
ar: ومن كثر كلامه كثر خطوه, ومن كثر خطوه قل حياوه, ومن قل حياوه قل ورعه, ومن قل ورعه مات قلبه, ومن مات قلبه دخل النار.
en: He who speaks more commits more errors. He who commits more errors becomes shameless. He who is shameless will have less fear of Allāh. He whose fear of Allāh is less, his heart dies. He whose heart dies enters the Fire.

t: Self-reflection and contentment in Islamic teachings
ar: ومن نظر في عيوب الناس فانكرها ثم رضيها لنفسه فذاك الاحمق بعينه.
en: He who observes the shortcomings of others and disapproves of them and then accepts them for himself is definitely a fool.

t: Self-reflection and contentment in Islamic teachings
ar: والقناعه مال لا ينفد, ومن اكثر من ذكر الموت رضي من الدنيا باليسير, ومن علم ان كلامه من عمله قل كلامه الا فيما يعنيه.
en: Contentment is a capital that does not dwindle. He who remembers death much is satisfied with small favours in this world. He who knows that his speech is also a part of his action speaks less except where he has some purpose.

t: Relief follows extreme hardship
ar: ۳۵۱. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): عند تناهي الشده تكون الفرجه, وعند تضايق حلق البلاء يكون الرخاء.
en: 351. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: At the extremity of hardship comes relief, and at the tightening of the chains of tribulation comes ease.

t: Balancing family devotion with spiritual priorities
ar: ۳۵۲. وقال (عليه ‏السلام) لبعض اصحابه : لا تجعلن اكثر شغلك باهلك وولدك: فان يكن اهلك وولدك اولياء الله, فان الله لا يضيع اولياءه, وان يكونوا اعداء الله, فما همك وشغلك باعداء الله?
en: 352. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said to one of his companions: Do not devote much of your activity to your wife and your children, because if your wife and children are lovers of Allāh then He will not leave His lovers uncared for, and if they be enemies of Allāh then why should you worry and keep yourself busy about the enemies of Allāh?

t: Congratulating on a birth with gratitude to Allah
ar: ۳۵۴. وهنا بحضرته عليه السلام رجل رجلا بغلام ولد له فقال له: ليهنيك الفارس. فقال (عليه السلام) :
en: 354. Someone congratulated another person in the presence of Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, on the birth of a son saying: Congratulations for getting a rider of horses. Then Amīr al-mu’minin said:

t: Congratulating on a birth with gratitude to Allah
ar: لا تقل ذلك, ولكن قل: شكرت الواهب, وبورك لك في الموهوب, وبلغ اشده, ورزقت بره.
en: Do not say so; but say: You have occasion to be grateful to Allāh, the Giver, and be blessed with what you have been given. May he attain full life and may you be blessed with His devotion.

t: Livelihood arrives from unexpected sources
ar: ۳۵۶. وقيل له (عليه ‏السلام) لو سد علي رجل باب بيته, وترك فيه من اين كان ياتيه رزقه? فقال (عليه السلام):
en: 356. It was said to Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him: If a man is left in his house and the door is closed, from where will his livelihood reach him. And he replied:

t: Livelihood arrives from unexpected sources
ar: من حيث ياتيه اجله.
en: From whatever way his death reaches him.

t: Grief and the transient nature of life
ar: ۳۵۷. وعزي (عليه السلام) قوما عن ميت مات لهم فقال (عليه السلام):
en: 357. Condoling with people among whom one had died, Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said:

t: Grief and the transient nature of life
ar: ان هذا الامر ليس لكم بدا, ولا اليكم انتهي, وقد كان صاحبكم هذا يسافر, فعدوه في بعض اسفاره, فان قدم عليكم والا قدمتم عليه.
en: This thing has not started with you nor does it end with you. This fellow of yours was used to journeying and therefore it is better to think him still to be journeying. Either he will rejoin you or else you will rejoin him.

t: Renouncing worldly desires and self-discipline
ar: ۳۵۹. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): يا اسري الرغبه اقصروا فان المعرج علي الدنيا لا يروعه منها الا صريف انياب الحدثان.
en: 359. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: O’ slaves of desires, cut them short because he who leans on the world gets nothing out of it except the pain of hardships.

t: Renouncing worldly desires and self-discipline
ar: ايها الناس تولوا من انفسكم تاديبها و اعدلوا بها عن ضراوه عاداتها.
en: O’ people, take upon yourselves your own training and turn away from the dictates of your natural inclinations.

t: Interpreting speech with positive intent
ar: ۳۶۰. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): لا تظنن بكلمه خرجت من احد سوءا, وانت تجد لها في الخير محتملا.
en: 360. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Do not regard an expression uttered by any person as evil if you can find it capable of bearing some good.

t: Avoiding quarrels to preserve esteem
ar: ٣٦٢. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من ضن بعرضه فليدع المراء.
en: 362. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: He who is jealous of his esteem should keep from quarrelling.

t: Desire prolongation ruins actions
ar: ٣٦. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من اطال الامل اساء العمل.
en: 36. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Whoever prolongs his desire ruins his actions.

t: Haste and delay as folly
ar: ٣٦٣. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): من الخرق المعاجله قبل الامكان, والاناه بعد الفرصه.
en: 363. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: To make haste before the proper time or to delay after a proper opportunity, in either case is folly.

t: Self-improvement through observing others' flaws
ar: ٣٦٥. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الفكر مراه صافيه, والاعتبار منذر ناصح, وكفي ادبا لنفسك تجنبك ما كرهته لغيرك.
en: 365. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Imagination is a clear mirror, and the taking of lessons (from things around) provides warning and counsel. It is enough for improving yourself that you should avoid what you consider bad in others.

t: Knowledge necessitates action
ar: ٣٦٦. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): العلم مقرون بالعمل فمن علم عمل, والعلم يهتف بالعمل فان اجابه والا ارتحل عنه.
en: 366. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Knowledge is associated with action. Therefore, he who knows should act, because knowledge calls for action; if there is a response well and good, otherwise it (i.e., knowledge) departs from him.

t: Worldly wealth as fleeting and perilous
ar: ٣٦٧. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): يا ايها الناس, متاع الدنيا حطام موبي فتجنبوا مرعاه! قلعتها احظي من طمانينتها وبلغتها ازكي من ثروتها, حكم علي مكثر منها بالفاقه واعين من غني عنها بالراحه, من راقه زبرجها اعقبت ناظريه كمها ومن استشعرالشغف بها ملات ضميره اشجانا, لهن رقص علي سويداء قلبه هم يشغله, وغم يحزنه, كذلك حتي يوخذ بكظمه فيلقي بالفضاء, منقطعا ابهراه, هينا علي الله فناوه, وعلي الاخوان القاوه.
en: 367. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: O’ people, the wealth of this world is broken orts that brings an epidemic; therefore keep off this grazing land, leaving it is a greater favour than peacefully staying in it, and its part enough for subsistence is more blissful than its riches. Destitution has been ordained for those who are rich here, while comfort has been destined for those who keep away from it. If a person is attracted by its dazzle, it blinds both his eyes; and if a person acquires eagerness towards it, then it fills his heart with griefs which keep alternating in the black part of his heart, some grief worrying him and another giving him pain. This goes on till the suffocation of death overtakes him. He is flung in the open while both the shrines of his heart are severed. It is easy for Allāh to cause him to die and for his comrades to put him in the grave.

t: Worldly wealth as fleeting and perilous
ar: وانما ينظر المومن الي الدنيا بعين الاعتبار ويقتات منها ببطن الاضطرار ويسمع فيها باذن المقت والابغاض, ان قيل اثري قيل اكدي! وان فرح له بالبقاء حزن له بالفناء! هذا ولم ياتهم يوم فيه يبلسون.
en: The believer sees the world with eyes that derive instruction, and takes from it food enough for his barest needs. He hears in it with ears of hatred and enmity. If it is said (about someone) that he has become rich, it is also said that he has turned destitute; and if pleasure is felt on one’s living, grief is felt over his death. This is the position, although the day has not yet approached when they will be disheartened.

t: Decline of Quranic understanding and mosque guidance
ar: ۳۶۹. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): ياتي علي الناس زمان لا يبقي فيهم من القران الا رسمه, ومن الاسلام الا اسمه, ومساجدهم يوميذ عامره من البناء, خراب من الهدي, سكانها وعمارها شر اهل الارض, منهم تخرج الفتنه, واليهم تاوي الخطييه, يردون من شذ عنها فيها, ويسوقون من تاخر عنها اليها, يقول الله سبحانه فبي حلفت لابعثن علي اوليك فتنه تترك الحليم فيها حيران, وقد فعل, ونحن نستقيل الله عثره الغفله.
en: 369. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: A time will come when nothing will remain of the Qur’ān except its writing, and nothing of Islam except its name. The mosques in those days will be busy with regards to construction but desolate with regard to guidance. Those staying in them and those visiting them will be the worst of all on earth. From them mischief will spring up and towards them all wrong will turn. If anyone isolates himself from it (mischief) they will fling him back to it and if anyone steps back from it they will push him towards it. Says Allāh, the Glorified, (in ḥadīth qudsī — i.e., the tradition in which Allāh Himself speaks) : I swear by Myself that I shall send upon them an evil wherein the endurer would be bewildered, and He would do so. We seek Allāh’s pardon from stumbling through neglect.

t: Islamic virtues and moral guidance
ar: ۳۷۱. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): لا شرف اعلي من الاسلام, ولا عز اعز من التقوي, ولا معقل احسن من الورع, ولا شفيع انجح من التوبه, ولا كنز اغني من القناعه, ولا مال اذهب للفاقه من الرضي بالقوت, ومن اقتصر علي بلغه الكفاف فقد انتظم الراحه وتبوا خفض الدعه والرغبه مفتاح النصب, ومطيه التعب, والحرص والكبر والحسد دواع الي التقحم في الذنوب, والشر جامع مساوي العيوب.
en: 371. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: There is no distinction higher than Islam; no honour more honourable than fear of Allāh; no asylum better than self restraint; no intercessor more effective than repentance; no treasure more precious than contentment; and no wealth is a bigger remover of destitution than being satisfied with mere sustenance. He who confines himself to what is just enough for maintenance achieves comfort and prepares abode in ease. Desire is the key of grief and the conveyance of distress. Greed, vanity and jealousy are incentives to falling into sins and mischief-mongering is the collection of all bad habits.

t: Four Pillars of Religious and Worldly Stability
ar: ۳۷۲. وقال (عليه ‏السلام) لجابر بن عبد الله الانصاري
en: 372. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said to Jābir ibn ‘Abdillāh al-Anṣārī:

t: Four Pillars of Religious and Worldly Stability
ar: يا جابر, قوام الدين والدنيا باربعه: عالم مستعمل علمه, وجاهل لا يستنكف ان يتعلم, وجواد لا يبخل بمعروفه, وفقير لا يبيع اخرته بدنياه; فاذا ضيع العالم علمه استنكف الجاهل ان يتعلم, واذا بخل الغني بمعروفه باع الفقير اخرته بدنياه.
en: O’ Jābir, the mainstay of religion and the world are four persons: The scholar who acts on his knowledge; the ignorant who does not feel ashamed of learning; the generous who is not niggardly in his favours; and the destitute who does not sell his next life for his worldly benefits. Consequently, when the scholar wastes his knowledge, the ignorant feels shame in learning; and when the generous is niggardly with his favours, the destitute sells his next life for the worldly benefits.

t: Four Pillars of Religious and Worldly Stability
ar: يا جابر من كثرت نعم الله عليه كثرت حوايج الناس اليه فمن قام لله فيها بما يجب فيها عرضها للدوام والبقاء ومن لم يقم فيها بما يجب عرضها للزوال والفناء.
en: O’ Jābir, if favours of Allāh abound on a person the people’s needs towards him also abound. Therefore, he who fulfils for Allāh all that is obligatory on him in this regard will preserve them (Allāh’s favours) in continuance and perpetuity, while he who does not fulfil those obligations will expose them to decay and destruction.

t: Degrees of disapproving evil in Islam
ar: ۳۷۴. وفي كلام اخر له (عليه السلام) يجري هذا المجري:
en: 374. Another saying in the same strain runs as follows:

t: Degrees of disapproving evil in Islam
ar: فمنهم المنكر للمنكر بيده ولسانه وقلبه فذلك المستكمل لخصال الخير, ومنهم المنكر بلسانه وقلبه والتارك بيده فذلك متمسك بخصلتين من خصال الخير ومضيع خصله, ومنهم المنكر بقلبه والتارك بيده ولسانه فذلك الذي ضيع اشرف الخصلتين من الثلاث وتمسك بواحده, ومنهم تارك لانكار المنكر بلسانه وقلبه ويده فذلك ميت الاحياء.
en: So, among them (the Muslim community) there is he who disapproves evil with his hand, tongue and heart. This man has perfectly attained the virtuous habits. And among them there is he who disapproves evil with his tongue and heart but not with his hand. This man has attained only two virtuous habits but lacks one. And among them there is the third one who disapporves evil with his heart but not with his tongue and hand. This is the one who lacks the two better qualities out of three and holds only one. Then, among them there is also he who does not disapprove evil either with his tongue, heart or hand. He is just a dead man among the living.

t: Degrees of disapproving evil in Islam
ar: وما اعمال البر كلها والجهاد في سبيل الله, عند الامر بالمعروف والنهي عن المنكر, الا كنفثه في بحر لجي وان الامر بالمعروف والنهي عن المنكر لا يقربان من اجل, ولا ينقصان من رزق, وافضل من ذلك كله كلمه عدل عند امام جاير.
en: All the virtuous deeds including war in the way of Allāh as compared to the persuasion for good and dissuasion from evil are just like spitting in the deep ocean. The persuasion for good and dissuasion from evil do not bring death nearer nor do they lessen the livelihood. And better than all this is to utter a just expression before the tyrannical ruler.

t: Stages of moral decline in conflict
ar: ۳۷۵. وعن ابي جحيفه قال: سمعت امير المومنين (عليه السلام) يقول:
en: 375. It is related from Abū Juḥayfah who said: I heard Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, saying:

t: Stages of moral decline in conflict
ar: اول ما تغلبون عليه من الجهاد الجهاد بايديكم, ثم بالسنتكم, ثم بقلوبكم; فمن لم يعرف بقلبه معروفا, ولم ينكر منكرا, قلب فجعل اعلاه اسفله, واسفله اعلاه.
en: The first fighting with which you will be overpowered is the fighting with hands, thereafter with your tongues and then with your hearts. Consequently, he who does not recognize virtue with his heart or does not disapprove evil will be turned upside down. Thus, his upside will be turned downwards and his lowside will be turned upwards.

t: Divine justice and mercy in human judgment
ar: ۳۷۷. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): لا تامنن علي خير هذه الامه عذاب الله, لقوله تعالي: ﴿فلا يامن مكر الله الا القوم الخاسرون﴾
en: 377. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him said: Do not feel safe from the punishment of Allāh even about the best man in the whole community because Allāh, the Sublime, says: But no one feels secure against the plan of Allāh save the people (who are the) losers. (Qur’ān, 7:99).

t: Divine justice and mercy in human judgment
ar: ولا تياسن لشر هذه الامه من روح الله لقوله تعالي: ﴿انه لا يياس من روح الله الا القوم الكافرون﴾.
en: Again, do not lose hope even for the worst man of the community because Allāh, the Sublime says : Verily, despairs not of Allāh’s mercy but the disbelieving people. (Qur’ān, 12:87).

t: Advice on wisdom, character, and choosing friends
ar: ٣٨. وقال (عليه ‏السلام) لابنه الحسن (عليه ‏السلام): يا بني, احفظ عني اربعا واربعا, لا يضرك ما عملت معهن: ان اغني الغني العقل, واكبر الفقر الحمق, واوحش الوحشه العجب واكرم الحسب حسن الخلق.
en: 38. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said to his son al-Ḥasan: O my son, learn four things and (a further) four things from me. Nothing will harm you if you practise them. That the richest of riches is intelligence; the biggest destitution is foolishness; the wildest wildness is vanity and the best achievement is goodness of the moral character.

t: Advice on wisdom, character, and choosing friends
ar: يا بني, اياك ومصادقه الاحمق, فانه يريد ان ينفعك فيضرك. واياك ومصادقه البخيل, فانه يقعد عنك احوج ما تكون اليه. واياك ومصادقه الفاجر, فانه يبيعك بالتافه واياك ومصادقه الكذاب, فانه كالسراب يقرب عليك البعيد, ويبعد عليك القريب.
en: O my son, you should avoid making friends with a fool because he may intend to benefit you but may harm you; you should avoid making friends with a miser because he will run away from you when you need him most; you should avoid making friends with a sinful person because he will sell you for nought; and you should avoid making friends with a liar because he is like a mirage, making you feel far things near and near things far.

t: Miserliness as the root of all vices
ar: ۳۷۸. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): البخل جامع لمساوي العيوب, وهو زمام يقاد به الي كل سوء.
en: 378. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Miserliness contains all other evil vices and is the rein with which one can be led to every evil.

t: Divine predestination of livelihood
ar: ۳۷۹. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): يا ابن ادم الرزق رزقان: رزق تطلبه, ورزق يطلبك, فان لم تاته اتاك, فلا تحمل هم سنتك علي هم يومك! كفاك كل يوم علي ما فيه, فان تكن السنه من عمرك فان الله تعالي سيوتيك في كل غد جديد ما قسم لك, وان لم تكن السنه من عمرك فما تصنع بالهم لما ليس لك, ولن يسبقك الي رزقك طالب, ولن يغلبك عليه غالب, ولن يبطي عنك ما قد قدر لك.
en: 379. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: O’ son of Adam, livelihood is of two kinds: the livelihood which you seek and the livelihood which seeks you; if you do not reach it, it will come to you. Therefore, do not turn your one day’s worry into a year’s worry. Whatever you get every day should be enough for you for the day. If you have a whole year of your life even then Allāh, the Sublime, will give you every next day what He has destined as your share. If you do not have a year in your life then why should you worry for what is not for you. No seeker will reach your livelihood before you nor will anyone overpower you in the matter of livelihood. Similarly, whatever has been destined as your share will not be delayed for you.

t: Divine predestination of livelihood
ar: قال السيد الرضي: وقد مضي هذا الكلام فيما تقدم من هذا الباب, الا انه هاهنا اوضح واشرح, فلذلك كررناه علي القاعده المقرره في اول هذا الكتاب.
en: as-Sayyid ar-Raḍī says: This saying has already appeared elsewhere in this chapter except that here it is clearer and more detailed. This is why we have repeated it according to the principle laid down in the beginning of the book.

t: The power and peril of spoken words
ar: ۳۸۱. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الكلام في وثاقك ما لم تتكلم به, فاذا تكلمت به صرت في وثاقه, فاخزن لسانك كما تخزن ذهبك وورقك فرب كلمه سلبت نعمه وجلبت نقمه.
en: 381. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Words are in your control until you have not uttered them; but when you have spoken them out you are under their control. Therefore, guard your tongue as you guard your gold and silver, for often one expression snatches away a blessing and invites punishment.

t: Guarding speech and fulfilling divine obligations
ar: ۳۸۲. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): لا تقل ما لا تعلم, بل لا تقل كل ما تعلم, فان الله (سبحانه قد) فرض علي جوارحك كلها فرايض يحتج بها عليك يوم القيامه.
en: 382. Amīr al-mu’minīn, peace be upon him, said: Do not say what you do not know; rather do not say all that you know, because Allāh has laid down some obligations for all your limbs by means of which He will put forth arguments against you on the Day of Judgement.

t: Worldly folly, neglected deeds, and misplaced trust
ar: ۳۸۴. وقال (عليه ‏السلام): الركون الي الدنيا مع ما تعاين منها جهل, والتقصير في حسن العمل اذا وثقت بالثواب عليه غبن والطمانينه الي كل احد قبل الاختبار له عجز.
